paavan_purani.name: Paavan Purani
paavan_purani.summary:
A Texas competitor, Paavan Purani is on an upward trajectory, currently competing at the U17 level, with 17 entries across 7 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

What makes Paavan especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries).

Paavan has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 7 different players across the career:
  • Sanjana Thouda (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)

Paavan has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Paavan brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
paavan_purani.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Paavan Purani, from Texas, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-25. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Paavan has 17 career entries across 7 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Paavan saga began at 2023 South ORC with 2 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Paavan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 17 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Paavan has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Harshvardhan Vinoth Kumar (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 South ORC in BD U15 — we hope Neal Vijay has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Paavan has shown up 2 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Paavan has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Paavan has 11 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Paavan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in BS U15, which means Paavan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Paavan entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Paavan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Paavan keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Paavan has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Paavan, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Paavan. We will be here, taking notes.

paige_gu.name: Paige Gu
paige_gu.summary: Paige Gu has stepped onto the competitive stage with 1 tournament entry. Every champion's story has a beginning, and this is Paige's.
paige_gu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Paige Gu, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U11 level. A player with 1 career entry and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Paige is right on schedule. It all started at 2025 Peak Sports South OLC, and so far, the only thing Paige has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Paige saga began at 2025 Peak Sports South OLC with 1 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Paige is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Paige's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Paige is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

Paige is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Paige wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Paige, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

paige_xu.name: Paige Xu
paige_xu.summary:
Representing Illinois, Paige Xu has stepped onto the championship stage, currently competing at the U17 level, reaching the finals 2 times. With 35 entries across 12 tournaments, Paige is proving the ability to compete at the highest level.

Paige is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (4 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

Paige is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #4, a doubles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries).

Paige has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 13 different players across the career:
  • Eric Zhun Hu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (6 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Sahana Samarthy (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (4 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)

Paige has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Paige brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
paige_xu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Paige Xu, from Illinois, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Paige has 35 career entries across 12 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Paige saga began at 2022 Egret Midwest CRC with 2 entries and a best of 4th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Paige experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Paige has reached the finals 2 times — at 2025 NE MassBad OLC, 2025 Egret Midwest OLC — and lost every time. Winning every match in a tournament except the one that actually matters takes a special kind of talent, and Paige has it in spades.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Paige plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 35 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 13 of them, to be precise. Paige has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Paige's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Eric Zhun Hu: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Eric Zhun Hu has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-49 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15 — we hope Tao Peng has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Paige has shown up 6 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

One thing nobody can accuse Paige of is playing it safe. Paige has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17 — which means Paige has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' The results in those older brackets have been... character-building. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Paige has built a permanent residence on that line. Paige has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Paige has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Paige showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U15, which means Paige traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Paige entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Paige paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 35 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Paige. Paige just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Paige has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Paige. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Paige: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

Partha is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #2, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Partha has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Sanskruti Kulkarni (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Allen George — 1 events, 1 title together in BD
partha_teja_malladi.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Partha Teja Malladi, from Washington, currently competing at the U19 level. A player with 1 win across 9 entries in 3 tournaments. That is a 11% conversion rate, which means roughly 9 out of every 10 times Partha shows up to a tournament, Partha pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Partha saga began at 2021 NW CRC, where Partha burst onto the scene with a title in BD U19 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Partha has delivered on some of those promises: 1 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Partha promised and what Partha has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 1 title in 6 entries — a stretch where Partha actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Partha is 0-for-3. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Partha plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Partha is 0-for-3. Zero titles in 3 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Partha and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Partha needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Partha's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 9 entries, and 8 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Partha looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Partha's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Partha has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Partha. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

  • Luke Wen (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (5 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)
  • Ivy Chang (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (3 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)
  • Aanya Latne — a trusted GD partner (4 events, best: #3)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Pearl has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Pearl isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Pearl brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
pearl_li.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Pearl Li, from Illinois, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-9. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Pearl has 39 career entries across 15 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Pearl saga began at 2023 Midwest ORC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Pearl would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 6 times Pearl has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 6 times Pearl has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Pearl's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Pearl plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 39 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 13 of them, to be precise. Pearl has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Pearl's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Luke Wen: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Luke Wen has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U11 — we hope Lukas Ye has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Pearl has shown up 3 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

Pearl has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Pearl has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Pearl has 16 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Pearl showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U11, which means Pearl traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Pearl entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Pearl paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 39 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Pearl. Pearl just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Pearl has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Pearl. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Pearl: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Peter brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
peter_lei.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Peter Lei, from Illinois, currently competing at the U13 level. A player with 1 win across 17 entries in 8 tournaments. That is a 6% conversion rate, which means roughly 10 out of every 10 times Peter shows up to a tournament, Peter pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Peter saga began at 2023 Egret Midwest OLC with 1 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2025 Egret Midwest OLC in XD U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Peter's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Peter plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Peter is 0-for-8. Zero titles in 8 tries. The best singles result is a 3rd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Peter and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Peter needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Peter has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Peter's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U11 — we hope Taara Kt has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Peter's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

Peter has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Peter has 4 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Peter showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U11, which means Peter traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Peter entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Peter paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 17 entries, and 16 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Peter looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Peter's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Peter has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Peter. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

philip_chu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Philip Chu, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Philip has 12 career entries across 5 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Philip saga began at 2022 NorCal ORC with 3 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Philip plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 12 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Philip has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 NorCal ORC in BD U17 — we hope Aditya Renganathan has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Philip has shown up 2 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Philip has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Philip has 10 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Philip showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U17, which means Philip traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NorCal ORC in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NorCal ORC, where Philip entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Philip paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Philip keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Philip has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Philip, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Philip. We will be here, taking notes.

phillip_you.name: Phillip You
phillip_you.summary:
Out of California, Phillip You wears the gold from Junior Nationals — the single most important tournament for any American junior player in BD U13. That title represents the culmination of grueling competition against the very best in the country. With 5 career victories across 23 tournaments, Phillip has proven the ability to deliver when it matters most.

Phillip is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (4 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

A true triple-threat, Phillip competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 singles title, 3 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals champion (BD U13) • 1 ORC title.

With 17 different doubles partners over the years, Phillip adapts to anyone:
  • Sahasra Reddy (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Yian Yang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (4 events, best: #5)
  • Sophia Sun (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Kayden Hossain — a trusted BD partner (4 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)

Having moved up to U17, Phillip is navigating tougher competition at the higher age group. The adjustment is natural — and with the pedigree Phillip brings, a return to the top is a matter of when, not if.

Phillip has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Phillip has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 7 seeded entries across the career, Phillip consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 5 titles, Phillip is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
phillip_you.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Phillip You, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has stood at the top of the podium at Junior Nationals — the crown jewel of the American junior badminton calendar — and won BD U13. That is a real, verified, indisputable accomplishment, and no one can ever take it away. But here's what they CAN take away: any illusion that the rest of this resume is equally impressive. Because we are looking at 64 career entries across 23 tournaments, 5 total wins, and 59 losses. A 8% win rate that suggests the Nationals title was less a coronation and more a cosmic accident that Phillip has been unsuccessfully trying to replicate ever since. The story started at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC, and it has been a wild, inconsistent, deeply roastable ride from there.

The Phillip saga began at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC with 2 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2022 SoCal Arena CRC in BS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Phillip's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 2 titles in 5 entries — a stretch where Phillip actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Phillip is 0-for-11. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Phillip experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Phillip has reached 9 championship matches across this career. That is 9 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 9 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2024 SGVBC SoCal CRC, where Phillip entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U15, BD U15). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (7 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Phillip does own 5 actual titles, so it is not like Phillip has never won anything. But 9 finals losses versus 5 wins means Phillip is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Phillip plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 3 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Phillip can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 17 of them, to be precise. Phillip has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Phillip's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Felicia Ho: 8 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 8 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Felicia Ho has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BD U15 — we hope Kayden Hossain has emotionally recovered.

Of Phillip's 5 career titles, 3 came at local events versus 2 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Phillip feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. Then there is the seeding situation. Phillip has been seeded in 7 events and underperformed the seeding 4 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Phillip takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2022 SoCal CRC in BS U11, and finishing 5th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Phillip. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Phillip of is playing it safe. Phillip has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15, U17 — which means Phillip has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 3 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Phillip has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Phillip is not just losing locally — Phillip is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 8% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The You family travels cross-country so Phillip can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Phillip has 14 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Phillip showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U13, which means Phillip traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 Arena SoCal ORC, where Phillip entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Phillip paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 5 titles to the name, 64 entries, and 59 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Phillip looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Phillip's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Phillip has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Phillip. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

A Illinois competitor, Prabhat Mandava has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-3), currently competing at the U17 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Prabhat's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Prabhat is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (6 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Prabhat especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, 2 doubles titles, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (11 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (11 entries) • ORC top-3 finisher.

With 20 different doubles partners over the years, Prabhat adapts to anyone:
  • Aditi Gummadi (current primary partner) — 3 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (14 total events in XD, spanning 5 seasons)
  • Radhika Birur (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U17/XD (3 events)
  • Anirud Veldanda (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #2)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Prabhat has competed across U13, U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Prabhat has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 5 titles, Prabhat is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
prabhat_mandava.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Prabhat Mandava, from Illinois, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 76 career entries in 26 tournaments, Prabhat has 5 titles — a 7% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 14 entries where Prabhat drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 76 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Prabhat saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 7th. The first title would not come until 2021 Egret Midwest OLC in BD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Prabhat's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 2 titles in 21 entries — a stretch where Prabhat actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Prabhat is 0-for-13. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Prabhat experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Prabhat has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2022 Midwest CRC, where Prabhat entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U15, BD U17). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. Now yes, Prabhat does own 5 actual titles, so it is not like Prabhat has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 5 wins means Prabhat is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Prabhat plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 2 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Prabhat can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 20 of them, to be precise. Prabhat has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Prabhat's texts. The go-to partner is Aditi Gummadi (14 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17 — we hope Sanjanaa Shyam has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Prabhat's 5 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Prabhat entered 11 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Prabhat's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Prabhat and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Prabhat has shown up 11 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

One thing nobody can accuse Prabhat of is playing it safe. Prabhat has entered older age groups at 6 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Prabhat has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 5 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Prabhat has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Prabhat is not just losing locally — Prabhat is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 7% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Mandava family travels cross-country so Prabhat can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Prabhat has 31 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Prabhat showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17, which means Prabhat traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Prabhat entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Prabhat paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 5 titles to the name, 76 entries, and 71 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Prabhat looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Prabhat's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Prabhat has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Prabhat. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Prajwal entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. Over 5 seasons and 4 titles, Pranav has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
pranav_gyajangi.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Pranav Gyajangi, from Illinois, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 73 career entries in 27 tournaments, Pranav has 4 titles — a 5% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 17 entries where Pranav drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 73 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Pranav saga began at 2021 Egret Midwest OLC with 2 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2023 Egret Midwest OLC in XD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Pranav's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Pranav experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Pranav has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. Now yes, Pranav does own 4 actual titles, so it is not like Pranav has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 4 wins means Pranav is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Pranav plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Pranav can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 25 of them, to be precise. Pranav has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Pranav's texts. The go-to partner is Erica Chen Lin (7 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-49 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15 — we hope Erica Chen Lin has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Pranav's 4 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Pranav entered 10 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Pranav's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Pranav and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Pranav has shown up 3 times with a best of top-33. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

One thing nobody can accuse Pranav of is playing it safe. Pranav has entered older age groups at 5 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15, U17 — which means Pranav has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 4 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Pranav has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Pranav is not just losing locally — Pranav is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 5% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Gyajangi family travels cross-country so Pranav can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Pranav has 35 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Pranav showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in BS U13, which means Pranav traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Pranav entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Pranav paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 4 titles to the name, 73 entries, and 69 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Pranav looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Pranav's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Pranav has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Pranav. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Pranav. Pranav just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Pranav has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Pranav. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Pranav: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

pranav_vivek.name: Pranav Vivek
pranav_vivek.summary:
Hailing from California, Pranav Vivek has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-4), currently competing at the U17 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Pranav's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Notably, Pranav has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Pranav competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 singles title, a doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (7 entries) • ORC top-4 finisher.

With 12 different doubles partners over the years, Pranav adapts to anyone:
  • Rishabh Praveen (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (5 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)
  • Gabby XZ Xiang (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

Pranav has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Pranav has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Over 5 seasons and 1 title, Pranav has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
pranav_vivek.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Pranav Vivek, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-9. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Pranav has 38 career entries across 16 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 3% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Pranav saga began at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC with 2 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2025 CAN-AM Elite NorCal OLC in BS U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Pranav's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 1 title in 15 entries — a stretch where Pranav actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Pranav is 0-for-11. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Pranav plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. All 1 title came in singles — the discipline where nobody else can mess things up. In doubles? Zero titles. In mixed? Also zero. Either Pranav is a lone wolf who thrives in isolation, or — and this is our working theory — nobody wants to be the partner who has to explain the loss at dinner. And then there are the partners — 12 of them, to be precise. Pranav has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Pranav's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Rishabh Praveen: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Rishabh Praveen has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in BD U15 — we hope Dhruvan Karthik has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Pranav's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Pranav has shown up 7 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

Pranav has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Pranav is not just losing locally — Pranav is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 3% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Vivek family travels cross-country so Pranav can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Pranav has 26 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Pranav showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in BS U17, which means Pranav traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC, where Pranav entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Pranav paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 38 entries, and 37 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Pranav looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Pranav's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Pranav has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Pranav. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

With 14 different doubles partners over the years, Prathik adapts to anyone:
  • Sophia Li (current primary partner) — 5 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (11 total events in U15/XD, spanning 4 seasons)
  • Sahasra Reddy (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Andy Korlann (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events)
  • Vishnu Anand (current partner) — an ongoing partnership that keeps producing results: 2 titles across 7 events in BD/U13, spanning 4 seasons

With 15 seeded entries across the career, Prathik consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 5 titles, Prathik is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
prathik_nemani.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Prathik Nemani, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 78 career entries in 27 tournaments, Prathik has 5 titles — a 6% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 14 entries where Prathik drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 78 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Prathik saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 2nd. The first title would not come until 2022 NW ORC in BD U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Prathik's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 3 titles in 18 entries — a stretch where Prathik actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Prathik is 0-for-13. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Prathik experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Prathik has reached 8 championship matches across this career. That is 8 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 8 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2024 Bellevue NW ORC, where Prathik entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U13, BD U13). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (7 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Prathik does own 5 actual titles, so it is not like Prathik has never won anything. But 8 finals losses versus 5 wins means Prathik is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Prathik plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 3 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Prathik can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 14 of them, to be precise. Prathik has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Prathik's texts. The go-to partner is Sophia Li (11 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in BD U15 — we hope Andy Korlann has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Prathik entered 9 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Prathik's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Prathik and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Prathik 9 times, with a best of top-4. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any.

Prathik has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Prathik is not just losing locally — Prathik is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 6% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Nemani family travels cross-country so Prathik can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15). Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Prathik has 24 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Prathik showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U13, which means Prathik traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC, where Prathik entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Prathik paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 5 titles to the name, 78 entries, and 73 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Prathik looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Prathik's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Prathik has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Prathik. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

At Junior Nationals, Prutha has shown up 3 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Prutha, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Prutha. We will be here, taking notes.
