Representing Texas, Puneeth Kumar Anumala is a multi-time champion on the junior circuit, currently competing at the U17 level, capturing 2 titles across 7 tournaments and 17 entries. Each win is earned through bracket battles against hungry opponents — and Puneeth keeps finding ways to come out on top.

A true triple-threat, Puneeth competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 singles title, 1 doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

With 7 different doubles partners over the years, Puneeth adapts to anyone:
  • Aryan R Cimala (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events, best: #3)
  • Tanushri Thokala (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)

The 2022-2023 season tells a story of acceleration, with 2 titles across 6 tournaments — a clear sign that Puneeth is still ascending.

Puneeth has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Puneeth has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Puneeth's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
puneeth_kumar_anumala.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Puneeth Kumar Anumala, from Texas, currently competing at the U17 level. A player with 2 wins across 17 entries in 7 tournaments. That is a 12% conversion rate, which means roughly 9 out of every 10 times Puneeth shows up to a tournament, Puneeth pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Puneeth saga began at 2022 South Houston CRC with 2 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2023 Midwest OLC in BD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Puneeth's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2022-2023 season has actually been the best yet — 2 titles in 15 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Puneeth plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Puneeth can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. On the doubles front, Puneeth has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Aryan R Cimala (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 South ORC in BD U17 — we hope Aryan R Cimala has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Puneeth's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Puneeth is not just losing locally — Puneeth is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 12% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Anumala family travels cross-country so Puneeth can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Puneeth has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Puneeth showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U15, which means Puneeth traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 Midwest ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Puneeth entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Puneeth paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 17 entries, and 15 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Puneeth looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, is still figuring it out, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Puneeth's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Puneeth has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Puneeth. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.
